title: Berry on Carpet
stainChemistry: Classification: Tannin (anthocyanin pigments). Berry bonds to carpet through anthocyanin bonds via hydrogen bonding. Treatment strategy: acidic tannin spotter to break hydrogen bonds, followed by oxidizing bleach to cleave chromophores.
whyThisWorks: Berry is a tannin (anthocyanin pigments) stain — anthocyanin bonds via hydrogen bonding. The treatment works by breaking these bonds chemically so it can be blotted or rinsed away from carpet.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin via tamping brush. Tamp gently from edges inward. Flush with steam gun. Check progress — if pigment is fading, repeat.

2. Apply H₂O₂ 6% with a few drops of 28% Acetic Acid (diluted) to lower pH to ~4–5, accelerating oxidation. Tamp into stain. Monitor — over-application lightens dyed substrates. Spot-test on colored goods. Use steam to accelerate if needed.
3. Flush thoroughly with NSD on spotting board. Apply leveling agent to feathered edges. Extract with wet vac. Neutralize pH if acid or alkali was used in previous steps.
products: acetic acid (28% Acetic Acid (diluted) for pH adjustment)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold — heat sets tannin pigments permanently; always use cold or cool water
warnings: Using ammonia or baking soda — alkaline agents permanently darken tannin pigments
Rubbing instead of blotting — rubbing pushes the stain deeper into carpet fibers
Applying heat before stain is fully removed — heat sets stains permanently

steps: 1. Remove any excess berry by blotting with a clean white cloth. Press firmly and lift straight up — never rub. Work from the outside edges toward the center.
2. Flush the stain with cold water. Blot to remove as much pigment as possible.
3. Apply H₂O₂ 3% directly to the stain. Let it sit for 5 minutes.
4. Blot with a cloth dampened with cool water to rinse. Stack paper towels with a heavy book on top for 1–2 hours.
5. Air dry completely. Do NOT use heat — no dryer, no iron, no hot water — until you are certain the stain is completely gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If two home treatments haven't removed the berry, a professional cleaner with commercial-grade chemistry can often finish the job. Explain what you've already tried — it helps them choose the right approach and avoid repeating failed methods.
warnings: NEVER use ammonia, baking soda, or alkaline cleaners on berry stains — permanently darkens tannin pigments. This damage cannot be reversed even by professionals.
Avoid heat until the stain is completely gone — heat permanently sets most stains.

neverDo: Use ammonia or baking soda — alkaline agents permanently darken tannin pigments
Rub instead of blot — rubbing pushes stain deeper into carpet fibers
Apply heat before stain is fully removed — heat sets stains permanently
fiberSensitivities: Carpet is chemically durable. Standard spotting chemistry is safe. Always test on hidden area for colorfastness on dyed goods.

escalation.when: Stain does not respond after two full professional treatment cycles, or pigment has cross-linked with fiber dye
escalation.whatToTell: Berry stain on carpet, treated with tannin formula and H₂O₂ 6%, age unknown. Possible cross-linking with fiber dye present.

contextQuestions: Is the stain fresh or has it been sitting for more than an hour?
Has heat been applied (dryer, iron, hot water) before treatment?
What color is the carpet — light, dark, or patterned?
What type of berry — blueberry, strawberry, raspberry, blackberry?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh stains respond significantly better. Heat exposure before treatment reduces chance of full removal. Darker carpets may hide residual staining. Blueberry and blackberry contain the most concentrated anthocyanins and are hardest to remove.
